item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On the date of 5/4 it said that the package contained change, it is unclear to me if it contained only cash or if the D-CLASS got the icecream and the change and if so if it was in the same package.

Fun story +1 from me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one was pretty enjoyable overall, but I feel like

" If it is approached by someone who does not appear happy, the subject will feign hostility, emitting a low growl from its interior, and refuse to interact positively until the individual's demeanor changes. It is as of yet unknown if SCP-1386 will act out on its aggression if exacerbated"

Belongs somewhere in the description

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Multiple tone errors (this is a scientific document, it must use metric, not imperial), the danger is too tacked on, F's death is too over-the-top: 1386 could've just beaten them up with it's door until they paid. Also, this is just a 294 that dispenses ice cream, and the coffe machine did if better.
-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The article that got me into SCP. Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Edits in the last four years have greatly improved this piece! :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
6/6/12 - No test. Personnel D-███ [logged at 5/30/12] was found dead in their holding block at 07:40. Approximate time of death is 04:30. Autopsy shows that D-███ died of strangulation, and light bruising on the neck confirms this. D-███'s death has been marked as suicide as they did not share a cell with any other personnel and their cell door showed no signs of forced entry.

…wait. Which D-class was strangled? Was it the one who saw the freaky hand?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A very enjoyable SCP, I would love to see or help create a larger list of test taken on SCP-1386.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You had me at the title. "April fools" and "I.O.U one toy" made me laugh.
Congratulations on making it to the 100 mark.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yay, ice cream!

…

…

What. The. FUCK.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is my new favorite SCP. Good Job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh, I like this one at some points, but other points stuck out against it to me.

It is as of yet unknown if SCP-1386 will act out on its aggressions if exacerbated.

First off with this, I'm not sure why they haven't tested that out yet. I would be way more curious about what this truck does when you piss if off than if it can deliver the desired ice cream and give correct change.

Second off, doesn't shredding the doctor show some aggression acting out on its part?

currently located in an evacuated four-block neighborhood surrounded by a 10-meter-high reinforced concrete containment wall in ████████, ██, 8 kilometers from the nearest inhabited neighborhood.

Is there a reason it's allowed to drive around and not in a small cargo holder? It seems dangerous it shouldn't be let out, but not dangerous enough that it should be hard to get into a metal box if you had the right equipment. Unless it teleports. Anyways, this one is majorly nitpicking. :I

Doctor D████

Why are Doctor D and F doing the testing in person themselves? I guess this is just head canon as far as proper procedures, but it always seemed like D-Class are the way to go for dealing with anything. IDK, maybe I'm nitpicking again.

The rest of the tests kind of goes on and on for me, with just more random occurrences and weird outcomes happening. Could maybe be condensed into a collapsible report document or something. Ending seems odd, but you build up to it enough it's not completely out of left field.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That got creepy as hell at the end. I wonder if the hand is different every time or if he just saw what it really was and then it corrected the error.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personally, I don't care for that section at all. However, I'd be more willing to overlook it if the D-class involved was, as mentioned, the mute one who freaked out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No worries on the absence. People come and go at will here. That's odd that the edit adding the suicide is marked under your user name, although you don't remember making it. StevenThePotato probably ought not to have made his change, but it was related to removing the "as if it were thinking" line whereas the system shows you adding the last few reports, including the suicide.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Apologies for being absent for so long. I have been running this SCP over in my head for a while and took a hiatus from it to see if I still liked what I have here. Most of it, I do, but I also agree with most of the critique given here. I should note, however, the final edit with the suicide was not mine and I had no intention of something like that happening at all. I plan, over the next few days to weeks, to revise what is here and give a better idea of what I am trying to convey. I will also update another few weeks worth of data-logging, if not more. Again, I apologize for my absence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would agree with either clarifying which D-Class was strangled or not strangling them at all. Since their designations are blacked out, we can't tell which got ye olde stranglin', but it would make most sense if it were the D-class who saw the disturbing version of the hand.

I enjoyed this, but consistent with current criticism, the violent death of the researcher seems strange and tonally inconsistent. It's also somewhat strange that they would send a researcher into an untested environment before a D-class.

I would suggest dropping the after-the-fact strangulation and changing the violent, explodey, ice-creamy death into something less obviously sinister. Perhaps he just becomes lactose intolerant.

I think I like the idea that this is some kind of crotchety old man-type paranormal entity the most. It seems to enjoy dispensing ice cream—just on its terms.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …I like this, but I think the death at the end might work better if the D-class who got strangled was the mute one that saw a two-fingered dead hand. I'd also be fine with chopping that out altogether, since for the rest of the article, 1386 seems to be mostly harmless as long as you play by it's 'rules'.

item.title: Admin post
item.content:encoded:
Qwis: We appreciate you taking the time to think about the article, and give detailed feedback. This is the type of thing we are trying to encourage on the site, well thought out and written reviews. Please do not listen to Downs, as he does not speak for the site.

That said, I'm closing this specific thread. Anyone who wants to talk about the SCP may continue to do so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the main idea behind the sentient ice cream truck is great and I said as much. I commented on this one because I could not stop thinking about it. In fact this is my first comment on a SCP. I'm new to SCP, but I'm not a twelve year old - I'm a Senior Principal System's Engineer with a master's degree 20 years experience. I read nothing in the site rules about prohibition on offering input based on 'street cred'. I'm puzzled by the hostility, but not altogether surprised (after all this is the internet). I will concede that the input I provided is my opinion. I'm not responding as an admin or authority of any sort. I really liked this concept and wished a couple things read better for the sake of future readers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You don't have to be a great writer to be a great critic. Heck just look and see how many movie critics have made movies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
True, but he is not a credible source of critique, and it feels cheap to criticise when one has not succeeded himself.

E: Oh, he has. 1971. Well fuck you too, wikidot karma.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I personally was impressed with qwisatz's comment; not only did they bother explaining their thoughts in detail, but they did so without seeming excessively vehement or accusatory towards the author. (also, the decent grammar and punctuation made me happy. :P)

Granted, I don't necessarily agree with the points that were made, but it's up to the author to decide whether or not they think the advice is valid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting statement. As it is a writing site, this actually IS a place for him to critique and give advice. If the author chooses to use it is up for grabs, but it's not YOUR place to reprove him for doing what he should be doing. Frankly, the very fact that he's trying to offer advice and help is a positive sign.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're entitled to your opinion but it is neither your place to critique nor give advice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a humorous SCP, why is it not a –J?

Because. Just because it's funny does not mean that it is a -J.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The fact that this SCP is stuck in my head days after reading is an indication of a great idea. However, I have a number of problems with the details:

  1. This is a humorous SCP, why is it not a –J?
  2. Why are you blacking things out in the containment section? Agents won’t even be able to find the bloody thing because you hid the location! This is forbidden in the rules.
  3. After travelling to Asia, I learned that the universal method to inform customers of a price is to punch the price into a calculator and show it to the customer. This would have worked better for me because I’m distracted by the thought of the handwriting for some reason. How can it be very sloppy and legible? At least loose the “very”.
  4. The April fools incident appears too early. Punch lines come after some build-up.
  5. The part about the van morphing into to swallow Doctor F: did not like this at all. I was enjoying thinking of this as a complex mechanical entity (implied by the title) or possibly a supernatural entity in a normal van. This incident turned it into a cartoon in my head and I don’t like cartoons. I’m all for killing Doctor F, but just have him get run over, pinned to a guard rail or have a shotgun come out of that slot.
  6. The description of how the van gives change is really awkward, not clever and not funny. Think of a better idea. Keep the last sentence though, that part is good.
  7. Why haven’t we seen this flesh colored appendage earlier in the testing? There’s receipts and ice cream going out constantly – can’t someone look into the slot? Aren’t they curious? Why not stick a camera in there?
  8. The last bit about the suicide did nothing for the article. Since this is largely humor, you should end with a joke. The joke right above it is good, just delete the suicide and you are set.

I’m surprised the ratings are as good as they are given this critical community. I’m on the fence so I’m going to wait and come back later. Maybe it will improve.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, but am a bit confused about the last bit. A dead-looking hand came out, another D-Class said the hand looked fine, and then was suffocated in his cell? It doesn't seem to fit the theme of "sentient ice cream truck".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one a lot, reminds me of invader zim for some reason

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I, as well, throw my hat onto Safe classification, it follows a regular, predictable schedule and has no ill effects if it is left alone in its walled-off neighborhood. Euclid stands for unpredictability to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am shocked, appalled, disturbed, and quite impressed.

Upvotified. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked how the van will play songs for its own amusement when there aren't any customers around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I very much like that containment suggestion. It is both detailed and clinical in tone. I have changed the procedures accordingly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You've changed them around to make it more contained, but it still feels a bit off.

The Foundation would move it immediately on discovery and wouldn't wait until something happened. You could drop the note about the doctor incident in the containment procedures themselves and just work in that only D-Class can interact with it. The readers will still find the reason in the listing of experiments.

My suggestion would be to lead off with something like:
SCP-1386 is currently located at Research Site-52 in a 3 block recreation of a typical American suburban neighborhood surrounded by a 10 meter reinforced concrete containment wall.

Another alternative would be an abandoned town that the Foundation has a perimeter wall around, but there has to be something to keep the object from just driving off whenever it feels like.

But otherwise, nice SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How should I change them to make them more appropriate?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for your input. I think that changing the classification to Euclid would be better in this instance.

Changed the placement of location.

The inner ear is the area of the ear that detects waves and the differences in sounds. Normally, people will have bleeding ears from the middle ear due to the eardrum being ruptured. It is unusual for sounds to cause the ear to bleed, so it would have to affect the inner ear.

Removed quotation marks from dollar amount.

Please explain further on your problems with this note, and which it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I played around with this concept and did not care for it. It has given me an idea for a detail of another SCP, however. Thank you for the suggestion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I tossed that in there on a whim. I would be glad to remove it as it does seem a bit out of place.

I did consider giving the van bad spelling, but I prefer that it does not capitalize anything. If this is not exactly what you are referring to, please explain more clearly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like how this works but the whole "turn you into pink goo" think seemed too surreal. Until that point it seems more like a creature that thinks it's an ice cream truck, and then that happened and all I see is a creature that wants us to think its an ice cream truck.

Maybe instead it tries to run the guy over, or spits the money at him like a gunshot, or creates a startling noise that makes the doctor drop the peach pop (ruining it) or drags him inside and has him visible from the driver's seat, clearly distressed but unable to remove himself from the wheel until such time that he's "paid his tab?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seems kind of interesting, but, well… there's no REAL containment procedures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this is a very good idea, and close to being a good SCP. five things are bothing me:

a: if it's driving around some abandoned neighborhood blasting music under constant surveillance, and it eats people who don't pay, it's not contained, and certainly not Safe.

b: the location should be the first thing in the containment procedures.

c: the siren makes the inner ear bleed? what?

d: don't putting dollar amounts in quotes. it's totally unnecessary.

e: the last note is fucking awful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agree on both of the top notes. The bottom, I also agree with kind of. If it were a D-Class it'd be interesting.

WAIT! Side effect. When dispensing change, people feel awe at its ability to do so. Perfect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, also, I strongly recommend getting rid of the doctor being amazed at having received exact change.

That the van gives exact change, sure. That the doctor is HOLY FUCKING WALRUSTITS MY GOD WOULD YOU LOOK AT THIS SHIT IT'S INCREDIBLE no you don't understand IT GAVE ME EXACT CHANGE GLORY HALLELUJAH…

no. Get rid of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First, I'd like to congratulate you on taking every piece of constructive criticism into account.

Second, I have the odd feeling that this would be even better if, despite being meticulous about serving customers correctly and making exact change, this thing wasn't the world's greatest speller.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: After reading this, I have changed all doctors following Doctor F to D-class personnel. Doctor F and prior will remain as doctors from the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have changed the containment section. I hope what is written now will suffice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Noted. All redacted sections have been explained. I prefer to keep the log chronological, however.

item.title: Re: What are we preventing?
item.content:encoded: Noted and changed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really want to like this, but there are so many things wrong. Like others said, why are the containment procedures the way they are? The redactions kill this (why should I care about someone's handwriting when I don't know who they are?) Furthermore, this has tonal issues, but not in the usual way. It starts out sounding very sinister, then gets progressively more whimsical, then there's some expunged awfulness, and it's whimsical again. The advice you got earlier, about moving the expunged part later, is very sound (also, take out the expungement, my word).

Basically, this thing really doesn't seem anywhere near as harmless or whimsical as you're making it out to be, and that's giving me cognitive dissonance like what.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When making contact with SCP-1386, it is strongly advised to have at least twenty dollars of American currency in hand and in plain view of it.

My first thought was "so the driver is Slender Man?"

item.title: Re: What are we preventing?
item.content:encoded: Redaction in the containment procedures is generally frowned upon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The containment procedures aren't explained in the description or testing log — Why must researchers look happy? Why must large groups never approach it? The expungement is lazy and there's really nothing scary or unsettling about this at all…it's very similar SCP-261, but with ice cream.

I also don't get the bit about the penmanship resembling [REDACTED]. It seems like the compelling parts of this article aren't quite there, but could be. A tentative downvote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I upvoted for the Greensleeves part.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I got the one you missed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel exactly the same way. I miss that aspect of the site from years ago.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed this. I will say the [DATA EXPUNGED] left me thinking, and that is a good thing. Now I just find myself wondering a lot about what happened. Well done. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Does this have issues? Yes. Did I still upvote? Yes. Why?
I want my damn Neopolitan turkey-cheese-tomato-looking ice cream sandwich!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My main problem with this is the way they keep blithely sending researchers in to do the testing even after one of them has been killed. Apparently they're even still in a good mood. Even if they didn't start out testing with D-class (which they should have), after what happened to Dr F, they would absolutely ban anyone not D-class from interacting with the thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why does it "have" to be approached by no more than 5 people, and why do they have to appear happy? The truck wasn't relocated until the 4/17/12 incident, so I assume nothing BAD happens if these requirements are not met. Does it just not stop for you unless you meet the requirements? If that were the case, the requirements shouldn't really be a "containment" procedure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is cute but I can't upvote until the danger is a little more explicit and not [REDACTED].

My general advice for that is to move mention of the danger to the end of the article too. You can have all the weirdness and then comment on the danger it presents in a special section, even if chronologically the event happened before some of the other more light-hearted tests.

And that also gives you a chance to expand upon WHY personnel need to pretend to be happy when it comes by, which makes the reader go back and re-consider everything they've just seen with the new information that those researchers were faking friendliness because they were terrified of what the truck might do to them. I'd even be happy if the truck just tries to chase/run people over who don't pay, it gives me a lovely imagined scene where the truck gets set off and pins one of the researchers to a tree while the guards get called in to try to restrain it somehow.
